text.title: Writing Specifications: Process, Rules, and Common Mistakes
text.summary: The 9-phase process for writing spec.md, document hierarchy rules, SMART/INVEST criteria for requirements, and a common mistakes table.

rule.statement: A specification answers WHAT and WHY. A design answers HOW. An implementation plan answers IN WHAT ORDER. Never mix concerns across documents.
rule.rationale: Mixing implementation details into spec.md causes the spec to become stale when the design changes, and breaks the clean hand-off between phases.

example.code: Specification → WHAT and WHY? → Requirements, user stories, acceptance criteria Design → HOW? → Architecture, file structure, API contracts Impl Plan → IN WHAT ORDER? → Tasks, dependencies, token estimates

rule.statement: Load constitution.md and completed feature specs BEFORE writing any requirements.
rule.rationale: Requirements written without constitution context violate tech stack constraints. Reviewing done/ features surfaces patterns and prevents re-inventing resolved problems.

list.item: Phase 1: Load context — read constitution.md, features/done/, related specs in todo/in-progress/
Phase 2: Problem analysis — define WHO, WHAT, WHY, IMPACT using SMART criteria
Phase 3: User research — define minimum 2 personas with role, goal, pain points, context
Phase 4: User story mapping — backbone, walking skeleton (MVP), phase 2 stories
Phase 5: Use cases — for multi-step workflows, multiple actors, or complex validations
Phase 6: Functional requirements — SMART requirements with MoSCoW, FR-X IDs, US traceability
Phase 7: Non-functional requirements — measurable targets (no vague words)
Phase 8: Acceptance criteria — Given-When-Then covering all scenarios with specific values
Phase 9: Scope definition — explicitly list what IS and IS NOT in scope

rule.statement: Every functional requirement must satisfy SMART: Specific (one interpretation), Measurable (verifiable), Achievable (technically feasible), Relevant (traces to user need), and have a testable pass/fail criterion.
rule.rationale: Vague requirements cannot be tested. "System should be fast" is untestable; "response time < 500ms p95" is not.

example.code: FR-001: System should be fast and handle user requests efficiently.
FR-001: System SHALL return API responses within 500ms p95 under 100 concurrent users. [Must]

rule.statement: Every user story must include the "so that [benefit]" clause. A story without a benefit clause hides the value and cannot be prioritized.
rule.rationale: The benefit clause is where business value lives. Stories without it get implemented based on assumed value, which is frequently wrong.

example.code: US-001: As a user, I want to log in with my email.
US-001: As a registered user, I want to log in with my email and password, so that I can access my personal account and saved data.

antipattern.description: "System should be fast" as a non-functional requirement.
antipattern.reason: Not measurable. Rewrite as: "Response time < 500ms p95".
Requirements written without personas are not grounded in user context and tend to describe system behavior rather than user needs.
Unidentified requirements cannot be traced to design decisions or acceptance criteria.

antipattern.description: Out of Scope section is empty or lists only obvious exclusions.
antipattern.reason: Out of Scope must name specific features that were discussed and explicitly rejected; generic exclusions do not prevent scope creep.

antipattern.description: AC items are not testable: "system should handle errors gracefully".
antipattern.reason: Acceptance criteria without Given-When-Then and specific values cannot be verified by a test.
